title: Limiting Results
description: Don't load 1,000 rows if you only need the Top 10.

slides.title: The Top 10 List 🏆
slides.content:
# Stop downloading everything!

If you want to show a "Top 10 Leaderboard" in your app, it is a catastrophic disaster to run a query that downloads all 5 million players just so the app code can read the first 10.

We need to tell the database to literally *Stop Processing* after finding the first 10.

slides.title: The LIMIT Clause 🚫
slides.content:
## A hard cap

In most SQL databases (Postgres, MySQL, SQLite), you add the **`LIMIT`** clause to the absolute very end of your query.

`SELECT * FROM players ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 10;`

The DB sorts by score, perfectly stops at number 10, and throws away the remaining 5 million. Incredible speed!

quizData.question: Why is LIMIT incredibly important for application performance?

options.text: It prevents the database from performing any math
It cuts off the database from processing millions of unneeded rows, drastically lowering network transfer speeds and computing time
It ensures users can never access the database

quizData.explanation: Downloading gigabytes of unneeded row data over a network crushes performance. Only asking for 10 removes the bottleneck.

slides.title: SQL Server Exception 🏢
slides.content:
## Microsoft TOP

Not every database uses LIMIT! Microsoft SQL Server uses the **`TOP`** keyword directly after SELECT.

Microsoft: `SELECT TOP 10 * FROM players ORDER BY score DESC;`
Everyone Else: `SELECT * FROM players ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 10;`

*(For this course, we'll universally use LIMIT)*.

quizData.question: Which databases typically use LIMIT?

options.text: None, LIMIT is not a real command

quizData.explanation: LIMIT is immensely popular across open-source databases. MS SQL Server is the major outlier using TOP.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Find the absolutely most expensive single product in the `products` table.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT *
FROM products
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 1;
